note: Grounding corpus for the research-assistant panel. Built by scripts/80_build_corpus.py.

chunks.title: What the American Policy Atlas is
chunks.text: The American Policy Atlas is a teaching observatory for statistics built on every county in the United States: 3,144 counties across 51 states and DC. It teaches a full statistics sequence — reading a choropleth, distributions, conditional expectations, regression, statistical inference, spatial dependence, and Bayesian shrinkage — with every method computed in the browser from the same national dataset. The 1939 HOLC redlining story in Los Angeles, at census-tract resolution, is the flagship case study. Data come from the U.S. Census ACS 2018-2022, CDC PLACES, BLS, BEA, WorldPop, and the University of Richmond's Mapping Inequality project.

chunks.title: Where the data come from
chunks.text: Demographics and economics are American Community Survey 2018-2022 five-year estimates: median household income, poverty, education, race and ethnicity, unemployment, housing tenure and rent burden, and health insurance from subject table S2701. Health outcomes — adult diabetes, obesity, and high blood pressure prevalence — are CDC PLACES model-based county estimates. County and tract geometry is Census TIGER/Line 2023. The redlining grades are from Mapping Inequality (University of Richmond), digitizing the 1939 HOLC maps.

chunks.title: Choropleth classification (quantile, equal, Jenks)
chunks.text: A choropleth colors each area by a number, but the same numbers tell different stories depending on where the color breaks fall. Quantile classification puts an equal count of counties in each color, which flatters skewed variables like income. Equal-interval uses equal value widths and is honest about magnitude but can leave classes empty. Jenks finds natural breaks that minimize within-class variance. None is uniquely correct; classification is an assumption a good analyst states out loud.

chunks.title: Distributions, mean vs. median, and skew
chunks.text: A distribution is the full answer to how often each value happens. Across the counties, median household income has a median of $60,938 and a mean of $63,312. The mean sits to the right of the median because the distribution is right-skewed: a long tail of high-income counties drags the balance point upward. The median splits counties into two equal halves; the mean is the balance point. That mean-median gap is the skew, and it is why the choice of color breaks matters.

chunks.title: E[Y|X] and the binscatter
chunks.text: A conditional expectation, written E[Y given X], answers: for counties at a given income, what is the average diabetes rate? It is the most useful object in applied statistics. The atlas approximates it nonparametrically with a binscatter — sort counties by income, chop into bins, plot the average outcome in each bin — letting the data choose its own shape. Regression is just the straight-line summary of this same cloud.

chunks.title: Regression and what a control does
chunks.text: The atlas fits diabetes prevalence on county median income, then adds present-day poverty as a control. The income slope shrinks substantially but does not vanish. Much of income's apparent link to diabetes runs through poverty, yet an independent association survives: income and poverty are correlated, not interchangeable. A control lets the regression keep the part of each variable that the other cannot explain. This is description, not a causal claim.

chunks.title: Sampling distributions, confidence intervals, hypothesis tests
chunks.text: Treat all county diabetes rates as the population (mean about 13.6%), then draw thousands of random samples and record each sample mean. The resulting sampling distribution is nearly normal even though county values are skewed — the Central Limit Theorem — and its spread is the standard error sigma over root-n, not the population standard deviation. A 95% confidence interval is one sample's mean plus or minus about two standard errors. A hypothesis test asks whether an observed gap exceeds that yardstick. The atlas contrasts Census-South counties against the rest and finds a difference far past any conventional threshold: the diabetes belt is not sampling noise.

chunks.title: Moran's I and spatial autocorrelation
chunks.text: Tobler's first law: near things are more related than distant things. Moran's I measures this by correlating each county's value with the average of its neighbors (its spatial lag). Neighbors are counties sharing a border (queen contiguity), built from the full-resolution Census boundary file. For adult diabetes the atlas computes Moran's I = 0.603 (permutation p = 0.001), across 2,921 contiguous counties averaging 5.88 neighbors each. That is strong positive spatial autocorrelation: regression residuals pool on the map rather than scattering, which is why a single OLS line understates uncertainty and why spatial models exist.

chunks.title: Empirical-Bayes shrinkage and small-area estimation
chunks.text: A county of 500 people with a 20% diabetes rate is built on almost nothing. Empirical-Bayes small-area estimation pulls each noisy local rate toward the population-weighted national mean, in proportion to how little information the county carries. The posterior mean is a precision-weighted average: theta-hat = w*y + (1-w)*mu, with weight w = tau-squared / (tau-squared + v). Here v is the county's own sampling variance (large for tiny counties) and tau-squared is the genuine between-county variance, estimated by method of moments. Small counties move a lot; large counties barely budge. This is the Fay-Herriot logic behind official model-based small-area releases.

chunks.title: Counterfactual projection from conditional means
chunks.text: Sort counties into five equal groups by poverty rate; diabetes climbs steadily from the lowest-poverty quintile to the highest. The atlas asks a transparent what-if: if a place-based investment closed half of each quintile's gap to the healthiest group, how many fewer adults would have diabetes? It applies that reduction to each quintile and counts avoided cases, weighting by population. This is not causal proof — it is a counterfactual built openly on the conditional means computed throughout the atlas. Good forecasting shows its assumptions.

chunks.title: The 1939 redlining case study
chunks.text: The flagship case study maps the 1939 HOLC redlining grades of Los Angeles at census-tract resolution and lays them beside present-day health and economic outcomes. Tracts graded "D" (colored red, "hazardous") in 1939 still show measurably worse outcomes today. The atlas links this to a national literature — most directly Aaronson, Hartley and Mazumder's boundary-discontinuity estimate of what the HOLC maps caused — and treats the local gradient as one legible instance of the spatial clustering measured nationally with Moran's I.

chunks.title: How spatial contiguity is computed
chunks.text: Moran's I uses queen contiguity: counties sharing any boundary point are neighbors. Weights are row-standardized and significance comes from a 999-draw permutation null. Critically, contiguity is computed on the unsimplified TIGER geometry, not the simplified web map — simplifying boundaries breaks the shared edges and destroys the neighbor graph. Alaska and Hawaii are excluded because their counties have no land neighbors.

chunks.title: Reproducibility and the build pipeline
chunks.text: Every figure is reproducible from the scripts directory: script 11 assembles the national county layer from ACS and CDC PLACES, script 12 computes spatial dependence on the raw TIGER shapefile. Inference demos resample county values with a fixed random seed so every figure is identical across reloads. The California tracts and redlining case study retain their own tract-level pipeline (scripts 10 through 60).

chunks.title: Daniel Aaronson, Daniel Hartley & Bhashkar Mazumder (2021): The Effects of the 1930s HOLC “Redlining” Maps
chunks.text: The Effects of the 1930s HOLC “Redlining” Maps by Daniel Aaronson, Daniel Hartley & Bhashkar Mazumder, American Economic Journal: Economic Policy 13(4): 355–392 (2021). Theme: Redlining & New Deal housing policy. The closest thing to a causal estimate of what the HOLC maps did: tracts that fell just on the red side of a boundary diverged for decades. The backbone of this atlas's redlining module.

chunks.title: Jacob W. Faber (2020): We Built This: Consequences of New Deal Era Intervention in America's Racial Geography
chunks.text: We Built This: Consequences of New Deal Era Intervention in America's Racial Geography by Jacob W. Faber, American Sociological Review 85(5): 739–775 (2020). Theme: Redlining & New Deal housing policy. Shows the maps did not just record segregation, they manufactured it, by writing “proximity to Black residents lowers value” into federal underwriting.

chunks.title: Jeremy S. Hoffman, Vivek Shandas & Nicholas Pendleton (2020): The Effects of Historical Housing Policies on Resident Exposure to Intra-Urban Heat: A Study of 108 US Urban Areas
chunks.text: The Effects of Historical Housing Policies on Resident Exposure to Intra-Urban Heat: A Study of 108 US Urban Areas by Jeremy S. Hoffman, Vivek Shandas & Nicholas Pendleton, Climate 8(1): 12 (2020). Theme: Redlining & New Deal housing policy. Formerly redlined neighborhoods run hotter today, by up to 7°C. A vivid case that a 1939 ink color still shapes the physical environment.

chunks.title: Nancy Krieger et al. (2020): Structural Racism, Historical Redlining, and Risk of Preterm Birth in New York City, 2013–2017
chunks.text: Structural Racism, Historical Redlining, and Risk of Preterm Birth in New York City, 2013–2017 by Nancy Krieger et al., American Journal of Public Health 110(7): 1046–1053 (2020). Theme: Redlining & New Deal housing policy. Preterm-birth risk climbs monotonically from grade A to grade D. The health gradient this atlas measures with diabetes, replicated at birth.

chunks.title: Douglas S. Massey & Nancy A. Denton (1988): The Dimensions of Residential Segregation
chunks.text: The Dimensions of Residential Segregation by Douglas S. Massey & Nancy A. Denton, Social Forces 67(2): 281–315 (1988). Theme: Segregation: measurement & causes. Names the five axes of segregation (evenness, exposure, concentration, centralization, clustering). Why one dissimilarity index is never the whole picture.

chunks.title: David M. Cutler & Edward L. Glaeser (1997): Are Ghettos Good or Bad?
chunks.text: Are Ghettos Good or Bad? by David M. Cutler & Edward L. Glaeser, Quarterly Journal of Economics 112(3): 827–872 (1997). Theme: Segregation: measurement & causes. Uses city-to-city variation to ask whether segregation itself harms outcomes. The template for treating a map as data, not decoration.

chunks.title: David M. Cutler, Edward L. Glaeser & Jacob L. Vigdor (1999): The Rise and Decline of the American Ghetto
chunks.text: The Rise and Decline of the American Ghetto by David M. Cutler, Edward L. Glaeser & Jacob L. Vigdor, Journal of Political Economy 107(3): 455–506 (1999). Theme: Segregation: measurement & causes. A century of segregation indices, 1890–1990, and the institutions that drove them up and then partly down.

chunks.title: John F. Kain (1968): Housing Segregation, Negro Employment, and Metropolitan Decentralization
chunks.text: Housing Segregation, Negro Employment, and Metropolitan Decentralization by John F. Kain, Quarterly Journal of Economics 82(2): 175–197 (1968). Theme: Segregation: measurement & causes. The original spatial-mismatch argument: separate where people live from where the jobs are and unemployment follows. Still the frame for South Central.

chunks.title: Elizabeth Oltmans Ananat (2011): The Wrong Side(s) of the Tracks: The Causal Effects of Racial Segregation on Urban Poverty and Inequality
chunks.text: The Wrong Side(s) of the Tracks: The Causal Effects of Racial Segregation on Urban Poverty and Inequality by Elizabeth Oltmans Ananat, American Economic Journal: Applied Economics 3(2): 34–66 (2011). Theme: Segregation: measurement & causes. Instruments segregation with the literal layout of 19th-century railroad tracks. A clean lesson in finding exogenous variation in a map.

chunks.title: Leah Platt Boustan (2010): Was Postwar Suburbanization “White Flight”? Evidence from the Black Migration
chunks.text: Was Postwar Suburbanization “White Flight”? Evidence from the Black Migration by Leah Platt Boustan, Quarterly Journal of Economics 125(1): 417–443 (2010). Theme: Segregation: measurement & causes. Quantifies how many white residents left cities per Black arrival. Movement, not just stocks, makes the modern map.

chunks.title: Sean F. Reardon & Kendra Bischoff (2011): Income Inequality and Income Segregation
chunks.text: Income Inequality and Income Segregation by Sean F. Reardon & Kendra Bischoff, American Journal of Sociology 116(4): 1092–1153 (2011). Theme: Segregation: measurement & causes. Rising inequality sorts families across neighborhoods, mostly by concentrating affluence. Connects the income choropleth to the segregation one.

chunks.title: David Card, Alexandre Mas & Jesse Rothstein (2008): Tipping and the Dynamics of Segregation
chunks.text: Tipping and the Dynamics of Segregation by David Card, Alexandre Mas & Jesse Rothstein, Quarterly Journal of Economics 123(1): 177–218 (2008). Theme: Segregation: measurement & causes. Finds discontinuities: once minority share crosses a tract-specific threshold, white population collapses. A regression-discontinuity classic on a map.

chunks.title: Raj Chetty, Nathaniel Hendren, Patrick Kline & Emmanuel Saez (2014): Where Is the Land of Opportunity? The Geography of Intergenerational Mobility in the United States
chunks.text: Where Is the Land of Opportunity? The Geography of Intergenerational Mobility in the United States by Raj Chetty, Nathaniel Hendren, Patrick Kline & Emmanuel Saez, Quarterly Journal of Economics 129(4): 1553–1623 (2014). Theme: Neighborhoods & mobility. Mobility is local and the rank-rank relationship is strikingly linear, exactly the conditional-expectation idea this atlas teaches in M3.

chunks.title: Raj Chetty & Nathaniel Hendren (2018): The Impacts of Neighborhoods on Intergenerational Mobility I: Childhood Exposure Effects
chunks.text: The Impacts of Neighborhoods on Intergenerational Mobility I: Childhood Exposure Effects by Raj Chetty & Nathaniel Hendren, Quarterly Journal of Economics 133(3): 1107–1162 (2018). Theme: Neighborhoods & mobility. Each childhood year in a better tract pays off linearly. Turns “place matters” into a dose-response curve.

chunks.title: Raj Chetty, Nathaniel Hendren & Lawrence F. Katz (2016): The Effects of Exposure to Better Neighborhoods on Children: New Evidence from the Moving to Opportunity Experiment
chunks.text: The Effects of Exposure to Better Neighborhoods on Children: New Evidence from the Moving to Opportunity Experiment by Raj Chetty, Nathaniel Hendren & Lawrence F. Katz, American Economic Review 106(4): 855–902 (2016). Theme: Neighborhoods & mobility. The randomized-voucher experiment that gives the neighborhood story a true counterfactual.

chunks.title: P. A. P. Moran (1950): Notes on Continuous Stochastic Phenomena
chunks.text: Notes on Continuous Stochastic Phenomena by P. A. P. Moran, Biometrika 37(1/2): 17–23 (1950). Theme: Spatial statistics & geography. The origin of Moran's I, the statistic this atlas uses to prove that nearby tracts resemble each other more than chance allows.

chunks.title: Luc Anselin (1995): Local Indicators of Spatial Association—LISA
chunks.text: Local Indicators of Spatial Association—LISA by Luc Anselin, Geographical Analysis 27(2): 93–115 (1995). Theme: Spatial statistics & geography. Breaks a global Moran's I into per-tract contributions, the math behind hot-spot and cold-spot maps.

chunks.title: Waldo R. Tobler (1970): A Computer Movie Simulating Urban Growth in the Detroit Region
chunks.text: A Computer Movie Simulating Urban Growth in the Detroit Region by Waldo R. Tobler, Economic Geography 46(sup1): 234–240 (1970). Theme: Spatial statistics & geography. Source of the First Law of Geography: everything relates to everything, but near things more so. The one sentence every spatial model assumes.

chunks.title: Paul Krugman (1991): Increasing Returns and Economic Geography
chunks.text: Increasing Returns and Economic Geography by Paul Krugman, Journal of Political Economy 99(3): 483–499 (1991). Theme: Spatial statistics & geography. Why economic activity clumps into cores and peripheries at all. The theory under the density choropleth.

chunks.title: Robert J. Sampson, Stephen W. Raudenbush & Felton Earls (1997): Neighborhoods and Violent Crime: A Multilevel Study of Collective Efficacy
chunks.text: Neighborhoods and Violent Crime: A Multilevel Study of Collective Efficacy by Robert J. Sampson, Stephen W. Raudenbush & Felton Earls, Science 277(5328): 918–924 (1997). Theme: Health, place & inequality. Introduces collective efficacy and the multilevel model, the reason we cannot treat tracts as independent observations.

chunks.title: Anne Case & Angus Deaton (2015): Rising Morbidity and Mortality in Midlife among White Non-Hispanic Americans in the 21st Century
chunks.text: Rising Morbidity and Mortality in Midlife among White Non-Hispanic Americans in the 21st Century by Anne Case & Angus Deaton, Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences 112(49): 15078–15083 (2015). Theme: Health, place & inequality. The “deaths of despair” paper. A reminder that health gradients move, and that descriptive statistics can be an alarm.

chunks.title: Raj Chetty et al. (2016): The Association Between Income and Life Expectancy in the United States, 2001–2014
chunks.text: The Association Between Income and Life Expectancy in the United States, 2001–2014 by Raj Chetty et al., JAMA 315(16): 1750–1766 (2016). Theme: Health, place & inequality. Life expectancy tracks income, and the gap varies by place. The clearest national version of the within-California health map.

chunks.title: Rebecca Diamond, Tim McQuade & Franklin Qian (2019): The Effects of Rent Control Expansion on Tenants, Landlords, and Inequality: Evidence from San Francisco
chunks.text: The Effects of Rent Control Expansion on Tenants, Landlords, and Inequality: Evidence from San Francisco by Rebecca Diamond, Tim McQuade & Franklin Qian, American Economic Review 109(9): 3365–3394 (2019). Theme: Housing policy in practice. A California-grown quasi-experiment: rent control helped incumbents stay but shrank supply. Policy impact estimated cleanly, the M7 goal.

chunks.title: Trevon D. Logan & John M. Parman (2017): The National Rise in Residential Segregation
chunks.text: The National Rise in Residential Segregation by Trevon D. Logan & John M. Parman, Journal of Economic History 77(1): 127–170 (2017). Theme: Segregation: measurement & causes. Builds a next-door-neighbor segregation measure from full census manuscripts back to 1880. Shows segregation is a 20th-century construction, not a constant.
